--- title: "Data Cleaning" author: "AD688 Team 1" format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: show execute: warning: false message: false --- ## Overview This page documents every cleaning step applied to the raw Lightcast job postings dataset. All subsequent research question pages load the resulting cleaned file (`data/lightcast_cleaned.csv`) so that our analyses start from a consistent, shared foundation. **Raw file:** `data/lightcast_job_postings.csv` **Output file:** `data/lightcast_cleaned.csv` ## 1. Load the raw data ::: {#2b83751a .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np import json import os RAW_PATH = "data/lightcast_job_postings.csv" OUT_DIR = "data" OUT_PATH = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "lightcast_cleaned.csv") os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True) df_raw = pd.read_csv(RAW_PATH, low_memory=False) print(f"Raw shape: {df_raw.shape[0]:,} rows x {df_raw.shape[1]} columns") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Raw shape: 72,498 rows x 131 columns ``` ::: ::: ## 2. Select the columns our team actually uses The raw file has many columns we don't need. We keep only those referenced across the five research questions (skills, industry, geography, remote work, salary, education, timing). ::: {#325d1c5a .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} keep_cols = [ # Identifiers & timing "ID", "POSTED", "EXPIRED", "DURATION", # Job info "TITLE_NAME", "ONET_NAME", "NAICS2_NAME", "NAICS_2022_2_NAME", "COMPANY_NAME", "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME", # Skills "SKILLS_NAME", "SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME", "SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME", # Education & experience "MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME", "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE", "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE", # Geography "STATE_NAME", "CITY_NAME", "COUNTY_NAME", "MSA_NAME", # Remote work "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME", "MODELED_DURATION", # Salary "SALARY", "SALARY_FROM", "SALARY_TO", ] # Only keep columns that actually exist in the raw file available = [c for c in keep_cols if c in df_raw.columns] missing = [c for c in keep_cols if c not in df_raw.columns] df = df_raw[available].copy() print(f"Kept {len(available)} columns.") if missing: print(f"Note: {len(missing)} requested columns not present: {missing}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Kept 25 columns. ``` ::: ::: ## 3. Standardize column names Lowercase, underscore-separated names are easier to type and less error-prone across teammates. ::: {#72f0cf37 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df.columns = df.columns.str.lower() df.head(2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
id posted expired duration title_name onet_name naics2_name naics_2022_2_name company_name employment_type_name ... max_years_experience state_name city_name county_name msa_name remote_type_name modeled_duration salary salary_from salary_to
0 1f57d95acf4dc67ed2819eb12f049f6a5c11782c 6/2/2024 6/8/2024 6.0 Enterprise Analysts Business Intelligence Analysts Retail Trade Retail Trade Murphy USA Full-time (> 32 hours) ... 2.0 Arkansas El Dorado, AR Union, AR El Dorado, AR [None] 6.0 NaN NaN NaN
1 0cb072af26757b6c4ea9464472a50a443af681ac 6/2/2024 8/1/2024 NaN Oracle Consultants Business Intelligence Analysts Administrative and Support and Waste Managemen... Administrative and Support and Waste Managemen... Smx Corporation Limited Full-time (> 32 hours) ... 3.0 Maine Augusta, ME Kennebec, ME Augusta-Waterville, ME Remote NaN NaN NaN NaN

2 rows × 25 columns

``` ::: ::: ## 4. Parse date columns `posted` and `expired` come in as strings. Parsing them now lets every downstream page do time-based filtering without reparsing. ::: {#6535787a .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} for col in ["posted", "expired"]: if col in df.columns: df[col] = pd.to_datetime(df[col], errors="coerce") # Derived time features the team uses often if "posted" in df.columns: df["posted_year"] = df["posted"].dt.year df["posted_month"] = df["posted"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str) df[["posted", "expired", "posted_year", "posted_month"]].head(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
posted expired posted_year posted_month
0 2024-06-02 2024-06-08 2024.0 2024-06
1 2024-06-02 2024-08-01 2024.0 2024-06
2 2024-06-02 2024-07-07 2024.0 2024-06
``` ::: ::: ## 5. Drop rows with no posting date or no title A row missing both a date and a job title can't contribute to any of our research questions. ::: {#97dceb6b .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} before = len(df) df = df.dropna(subset=["posted", "title_name"]) print(f"Dropped {before - len(df):,} rows missing posted date or title. {len(df):,} remain.") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Dropped 44 rows missing posted date or title. 72,454 remain. ``` ::: ::: ## 6. Deduplicate Lightcast sometimes includes the same posting more than once. We dedupe on `id` when available, otherwise on the combination of title + company + posted date. ::: {#e3a3ee2c .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} before = len(df) if "id" in df.columns: df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["id"]) else: df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["title_name", "company_name", "posted"]) print(f"Dropped {before - len(df):,} duplicate rows. {len(df):,} remain.") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Dropped 0 duplicate rows. 72,454 remain. ``` ::: ::: ## 7. Clean salary fields Salaries have outliers (e.g., hourly rates mis-coded as annual, or placeholder values like `1` or `999999`). We clip to a sensible annual range and flag valid salary rows. ::: {#71cd730c .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} for col in ["salary", "salary_from", "salary_to"]: if col in df.columns: df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce") # Reasonable annual salary range for US professional roles df.loc[(df[col] < 20_000) | (df[col] > 500_000), col] = np.nan df["has_salary"] = df["salary"].notna() if "salary" in df.columns else False print(f"Rows with valid salary: {df['has_salary'].sum():,} ({df['has_salary'].mean():.1%})") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows with valid salary: 30,807 (42.5%) ``` ::: ::: ## 8. Standardize remote work categories `remote_type_name` has inconsistent casing and a mix of nulls. We collapse it to three clean buckets: `Remote`, `Hybrid`, `On-site`. ::: {#1a784fce .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} def standardize_remote(x): if pd.isna(x): return "On-site" # Lightcast convention: null means not flagged as remote x = str(x).strip().lower() if "remote" in x: return "Remote" if "hybrid" in x: return "Hybrid" return "On-site" if "remote_type_name" in df.columns: df["remote_category"] = df["remote_type_name"].apply(standardize_remote) print(df["remote_category"].value_counts()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` remote_category On-site 56570 Remote 15884 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ## 9. Clean skills columns Lightcast stores skills as JSON arrays inside string columns. The raw values look like `["Python", "SQL", "Machine Learning"]` (sometimes with extra whitespace or newlines). We parse the JSON properly so each row ends up as a clean list of skill strings — no surrounding quotes, no leftover brackets, no blank entries. ::: {#fda2dfef .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} def split_skills(x): """Parse a Lightcast skills cell into a clean list of skill strings.""" if pd.isna(x): return [] s = str(x).strip() if not s: return [] # Lightcast raw format is a JSON array — try that first. try: parsed = json.loads(s) if isinstance(parsed, list): return [str(item).strip() for item in parsed if str(item).strip()] except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError): pass # Fallback for rare rows that use pipe or comma separators. s = s.replace("|", ",") return [ t.strip().strip('"').strip("'") for t in s.split(",") if t.strip() and t.strip() not in ("[", "]") ] for col in ["skills_name", "specialized_skills_name", "software_skills_name"]: if col in df.columns: df[col] = df[col].apply(split_skills) # Quick sanity check print("Sample parsed skills:") for skills in df["skills_name"].head(3): print(f" {skills[:5]}{'...' if len(skills) > 5 else ''}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Sample parsed skills: ['Merchandising', 'Mathematics', 'Presentations', 'Predictive Modeling', 'Data Modeling']... ['Procurement', 'Financial Statements', 'Oracle Business Intelligence (BI) / OBIA', 'Oracle E-Business Suite', 'PL/SQL']... ['Management', 'Exception Reporting', 'Report Writing', 'Security Clearance', 'Interpersonal Communications']... ``` ::: ::: ## 10. Standardize geography State names and city names sometimes have trailing whitespace or inconsistent casing. ::: {#fbd7a7ec .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} for col in ["state_name", "city_name", "county_name", "msa_name"]: if col in df.columns: df[col] = df[col].astype(str).str.strip().replace({"nan": np.nan, "": np.nan}) ``` ::: ## 11. Summary of the cleaned dataset ::: {#485ebf8d .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(f"Final shape: {df.shape[0]:,} rows x {df.shape[1]} columns") print(f"Date range: {df['posted'].min().date()} to {df['posted'].max().date()}") print(f"Unique job titles: {df['title_name'].nunique():,}") if "state_name" in df.columns: print(f"States covered: {df['state_name'].nunique()}") # Top 10 most common skills — sanity check that parsing worked correctly print("\nTop 10 most common skills (sanity check):") print(df["skills_name"].explode().value_counts().head(10)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Final shape: 72,454 rows x 29 columns Date range: 2024-05-01 to 2024-09-30 Unique job titles: 5,719 States covered: 51 Top 10 most common skills (sanity check): skills_name Communication 31661 Data Analysis 28515 Management 24631 SQL (Programming Language) 21733 Problem Solving 18522 Leadership 17561 Computer Science 17287 Operations 14767 Project Management 13711 Business Process 13278 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#02835500 .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} df.dtypes ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` id str posted datetime64[us] expired datetime64[us] duration float64 title_name str onet_name str naics2_name str naics_2022_2_name str company_name str employment_type_name str skills_name object specialized_skills_name object software_skills_name object min_edulevels_name str min_years_experience float64 max_years_experience float64 state_name str city_name str county_name str msa_name str remote_type_name str modeled_duration float64 salary float64 salary_from float64 salary_to float64 posted_year float64 posted_month str has_salary bool remote_category str dtype: object ``` ::: ::: ## 12. Save the cleaned file ::: {#ebb56ed0 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Convert list columns back to pipe-separated strings for CSV compatibility. # Each teammate's page will split them back into lists on load. df_out = df.copy() for col in ["skills_name", "specialized_skills_name", "software_skills_name"]: if col in df_out.columns: df_out[col] = df_out[col].apply(lambda lst: "|".join(lst) if isinstance(lst, list) else "") df_out.to_csv(OUT_PATH, index=False) print(f"Saved cleaned dataset to {OUT_PATH}") print(f"File size: {os.path.getsize(OUT_PATH) / 1e6:.1f} MB") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Saved cleaned dataset to data/lightcast_cleaned.csv File size: 88.8 MB ``` ::: ::: ## How to use this in your research question page At the top of your `.qmd` file (e.g., `omar.qmd`, `industry_hiring.qmd`), load the cleaned file like this: ```python import pandas as pd df = pd.read_csv("data/lightcast_cleaned.csv", parse_dates=["posted", "expired"]) # Skills columns are stored as pipe-separated strings in the CSV — split back into lists: for col in ["skills_name", "specialized_skills_name", "software_skills_name"]: if col in df.columns: df[col] = df[col].fillna("").apply(lambda s: s.split("|") if s else []) ``` Every page now starts from the same cleaned foundation, and any future fix to the pipeline (done here in `data_cleaning.qmd`) propagates to the whole site on the next render.
